perception.who_is_this_for_a: A Disney trip planner who wants to quickly set up a specific restaurant alert without giving Disney login credentials, with later proof and pricing available below.
perception.who_is_this_for_b: A skeptical Disney dining visitor or expert who wants to understand what an alert looks like, compare proof, channels, coverage, pricing, and FAQs before acting.

reasoning: Candidate A better satisfies the core first-viewport obligation: users immediately see the problem, no-login reassurance, official Disney handoff, and an actual free alert setup form with restaurant, dates, party size, and email. It makes the primary conversion concrete rather than abstract. Candidate B has stronger explanatory proof, especially the sample SMS alert, metrics, alert channels, FAQs, and richer Pro/coverage detail for experts, but its first viewport delays the actual alert setup behind a single CTA. On mobile, A preserves the form and primary task directly in view, while B shows trust and CTA but requires more scrolling before a user can begin setting criteria. B is more content-complete as a marketing page, but A is more aligned with the stated decisive requirement: safely and quickly start a free Disney dining alert.
blocking_visual_issue_a: The page is visually plain and somewhat sparse, with limited emotional warmth or sample-alert proof in the first viewport.
blocking_visual_issue_b: The first viewport does not expose the actual free alert setup fields, so the primary task is less immediately actionable.
